coredata.dc:title: pH-responsive mesoporous ZSM-5 zeolites/chitosan core-shell nanodisks loaded with doxorubicin against osteosarcoma
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Fan
Wen, Xi
Ke, Qin-Fei
Xie, Xue-Tao
Guo, Ya-Ping
coredata.dc:description: Oral or intravenous chemotherapy is an important strategy to treat metastatic cancer, but it may cause systemic toxicity for healthy tissue. Herein, we for the first time fabricated mesoporous ZSM-5 zeolites/chitosan core-shell nanodisks loaded with doxorubicin (ZSM-5/CS/DOX) as drug delivery systems against osteosarcoma. The mesoporous ZSM-5 zeolites exhibited disk-like shapes with thicknesses of 100nm and diameters of 300nm, and the mesopores with pore sizes of 3.75nm were originated from desilication treatment. The pH-responsive ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks possessed a great drug loading efficiency of 97.7%, and their controlled release trends of DOX were fitted well with the Korsmeyer-Peppas model. The DOX could be efficiently released the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks after cellular endocytosis and induced cancer cells apoptosis. Moreover, the pH-responsive drug carriers led to efficient tumor inhibition with low side effects, especially cardiac toxicity, as confirmed by pharmacokinetic study, serological examination and H&E staining assays. Therefore, the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks are a promising pH-responsive drug carrier for targeted cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZSM-5 zeolite
Chitosan
pH-responsive
Osteosarcoma
Cytotoxicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and drug release of ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks
Isolation of hBMSCs and cell culture
Osteogenic induction and alizarin red staining
Pharmacokinetics and cardiotoxicity study in normal rat
Morphology and porous structure of mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks
Morphology and porous structure of mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks
Drug delivery performances of mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks
Characterization and cytotoxicity assay of hBMSCs
In vitro cytotoxicity studies of MG63

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM and (b) TEM images of ZSM-5 nanodisks.
(a) SEM image, (b) EDS spectrum, (c, d) low-resolution TEM images, (e) high-resolution TEM image and (f) SAED pattern of mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distribution curve of mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks.
XRD patterns of samples: (a) CS powders, (b) mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks and (c) mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks.
XRD patterns of samples: (a) CS powders, (b) mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks and (c) mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks.

(a) In vitro cumulative release ratio of DOX from mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks, (b) cumulative release concentrations of DOX from mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks; and (c) cumulative release concentrations of DOX versus one-sixth power of time for mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks. The pH values of release media were 5.5, 6.0 and 7.4, respectively.

(a) Surface markers of hBMSCs were examined by flow cytometric analysis. The red curves indicate the result of negative control; while the blue curves indicate the results of hBMSCs samples with different antibodies. (b) osteogenic induction and Alizarin Red staining of hBMSCs. (c) Cytotoxicity of free DOX, ZSM-5 and ZSM-5/CS/DOX against hBMSCs for 1d and 5d; Means±SD (n =3). *p <0.05. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence microscopic images of MG63 which cultured with ZSM-5/CS/DOX for 1day.

(a) Cytotoxicity of DOX, ZSM-5 and ZSM-5/CS/DOX conjugate against MG63 for 24h. Means±SD (n =3). *p <0.05. #ZSM-5 does not contain DOX, and the mass ratio is the same as HM-ZSM-5/CS/DOX. (b) Toxicity of ZSM-5 and ZSM-5/CS/DOX to MG63 in different pH. Means±SD (n =3). *p <0.05.

Representative CLSM images of ZSM-5, DOX and ZSM-5/CS/DOX after culture for 12h, and 24h. Blue: cell nuclei. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) DOX concentrations in serum and heart; (b) H&E staining images of the major organs of mice with different treatments.
(a) CK, (b) MDA and (c) LDH levels after the intravenous injection of ZSM-5/CS/DOX and free DOX.
Proposed schematic model showing ZSM/CS/DOX preparation, intracellular drug release and drug distribution experiments in animals.

head.ce:title: pH-responsive mesoporous ZSM-5 zeolites/chitosan core-shell nanodisks loaded with doxorubicin against osteosarcoma

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Shanghai Jiao Tong University Affiliated Sixth People's Hospital, Shanghai 200233, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Jiao Tong University Affiliated Sixth People's Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Education Ministry Key Lab of Resource Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, Shanghai Normal University, Shanghai 200234, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Education Ministry Key Lab of Resource Chemistry and Shanghai Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oral or intravenous chemotherapy is an important strategy to treat metastatic cancer, but it may cause systemic toxicity for healthy tissue. Herein, we for the first time fabricated mesoporous ZSM-5 zeolites/chitosan core-shell nanodisks loaded with doxorubicin (ZSM-5/CS/DOX) as drug delivery systems against osteosarcoma. The mesoporous ZSM-5 zeolites exhibited disk-like shapes with thicknesses of 100nm and diameters of 300nm, and the mesopores with pore sizes of 3.75nm were originated from desilication treatment. The pH-responsive ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks possessed a great drug loading efficiency of 97.7%, and their controlled release trends of DOX were fitted well with the Korsmeyer-Peppas model. The DOX could be efficiently released the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks after cellular endocytosis and induced cancer cells apoptosis. Moreover, the pH-responsive drug carriers led to efficient tumor inhibition with low side effects, especially cardiac toxicity, as confirmed by pharmacokinetic study, serological examination and H&E staining assays. Therefore, the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks are a promising pH-responsive drug carrier for targeted cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Osteosarcoma is one of the most common primary malignant bone tumors. It represents the second highest risk of cancer-related death in children and adolescents [1,2,3]. The primary sites of osteosarcoma locate mainly at distal humeri, distal femurs and proximal tibias [1]. The standard therapies of osteosarcoma are composed of surgical resection, radiotherapy, phototherapy and chemotherapy [4,5,6,7,8]. The surgical resection can effectively dislodge limited tumor, but the surrounding healthy tissue is partly damaged to cause large bone defects [9]. In addition, chemotherapy either intravenously or orally is employed to treat the different stages of metastatic cancer. Doxorubicin (DOX) is a broad spectrum antitumor drug that can intercalate with the DNA of tumor cells, inhibit the proliferation of tumor cells and accelerate their death [10]. Its adverse effects on healthy cells and tissues may take place during chemotherapy process [11,12]. Hence, it is urgent to develop novel drug delivery systems, and the controlled release of DOX improves the therapeutic efficacy at tumor-targeted site without systemic toxicity.

Over the last decade, inorganic nanoparticles [13–17], metal–organic framework [18], poly(9,9-dioctylfluorene-alt-benzothiadiazole) polymer dots [19], folate-targeted gold nanorods [20–23], hybrid materials [24,25], natural materials [26,27] and carbon dots [28,29] have been proposed as drug delivery systems for treating osteosarcoma. Notably, ZSM-5 zeolites are alternative candidates for bone filling materials and drug delivery systems due to their ordered microporous structure, excellent biocompatibility and osteoinductivity [30,31]. Both in vitro and in vivo tests indicate that ZSM-5 zeolites exhibit the ability to promote the formation of mineralized matrix [32,33]. The released Si element from ZSM-5 zeolites contributes to osteoblast differentiation by antagonizing NF-κB activation [34]. Moreover, the ordered micropores with parallel upright channels of 0.51nm×0.55nm and the zigzag channels of 0.53nm×0.56nm can act as drug delivery channels [35,36]. Unfortunately, the sole micropores have so small pore sizes that the diffusion limitations may be imposed for chemotherapeutic macromolecules. The cooperation of mesopores in zeolites is an alternative strategy by desilication technology or template method [37,38]. The hierarchically porous ZSM-5 zeolites including microporous and mesoporous structures not only provide large pore volumes and great surface areas for loading drugs, but also facilitate the physical transport of chemotherapeutics molecules in/out the carriers. Previous works have reported the synthesis of hierarchical zeolites by a soft-templating strategy using amphiphilic organics [39], cetyltrimethylammonium [36], gemini-type organosilane [40] as structure-directing agents (SDAs). However, the removal of organic templates may destroy the mesoporous channels during high sintering or solvent extraction process, and even the residual organic surfactants cause toxicity to healthy cells or tissues. Fortunately, the secondary network of mesoporous structure in ZSM-5 zeolites is introduced by alkaline desilication [41,42]. After alkaline treatment, the surface areas increase from 40m2/g up to 250m2/g without damaging the integrate crystal structure [43]. In addition, the particle sizes of carriers play an important role in drug delivery performance and therapeutic efficacy. The chemotherapeutic agent-loaded nanoscale carriers exhibit the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, resulting in increasing the accumulation of drugs in tumors and reducing unwanted release in normal tissue [44,45,46].

As we know, common chemotherapeutics drugs have a great toxicity to normal tissues and organs. In order to decrease the side-effects of chemotherapeutics drugs, a chief issue is how to “switch off” the mesopores for the controlled drug delivery at lesion sites [47]. The fact that tumor cells present weakly acidic environment than normal tissues and cells provides a prerequisite for the design of pH-responsive drug carriers [48,49]. Recently, many pH-responsive polymers such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) [50], poly(2-(diethylamino)ethyl methacrylate) [51] and chitosan (CS) [52,53] have been used to functionalize drug carriers. The relative acidic environment around or within tumors is significantly different from the surrounding normal tissues. The pH-responsive nanocarriers release different amounts of drugs in different pH environments, thus achieving the drug targeted release function [47,54].

In this work, we firstly fabricated mesoporous ZSM-5/CS core-shell nanodisks loaded with DOX (ZSM-5/CS/DOX). The ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks possessed the following advantages including: (i) the mesopores and micropores in the ZSM-5 nanodisks contribute to great drug loading efficiency; (ii) the CS layers on the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks made the composite carriers possess pH-responsive performances and showed high drug release rate in the slightly acidic environment than in the neutral environment; and (iii) both the CS and mesoporous ZSM-5 zeolites exhibited excellent biocompatibility. In addition, in vitro and in vivo tests demonstrated that the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks could enter into cells by the process of phagocytosis, resulting in the good chemotherapy efficiency.

Hydrothermal method was used to prepare ZSM-5nanodisks by using 3-aminopropyl-trimethoxysilane (APTMS) as an organic additive. Aluminum isopropoxide, tetraethyl orthosilicate and tetrapropy-lammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) were successively added into deionized water under violent stirring. The molar ratio of Al2O3:SiO2:TPAOH:H2O was controlled at 0.5:400:11.5:1500. The precrystallization of the mixture was refluxed at 90°C for 20h, followed by adding APTMS. After further stirring for 6h, the precursor solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted hydrothermally at 170°C for 5d. Finally, the products (ZSM-5 nanodisks) were centrifuged, washed with deionized water, dried at 80°C overnight, and calcined in a muffle furnace at 550°C for 4h.

Mesoporous ZSM-5nanodiskswere converted from ZSM-5nanodisks by alkaline desilication. Briefly, 1.0g ZSM-5nanodisks were dispersed in 100mL Na2CO3 (0.6mol/L). The mixture was stirred at 65°C for 30min, followed by ice-bath treatment for 10min. The as-obtained precipitations were centrifuged, washed with deionized water, and dried at 80°C overnight. Finally, the products mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks were calcined in a muffle furnace at 550°C for 4h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and drug release of ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks
ce:section.ce:para: 0.20g CS powders were added in 25mL acetic acid solution (1.0vol%) under violent stirring, forming homogeneous CS solution. The pH value was adjusted up to 6.0 using0.5mol/L NaOH solution. 2.0g ZSM-5 nanodisks and 3.19ml DOX (0.1g/L) were added into the above CS solution. After stirring for 24h, the pH value of the mixture was adjusted to 7.4 by 0.5mol/L NaOH solutions. Finally, the products (ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks) were centrifuged, washed with deionized water, and dried in a vacuum oven at 37°C overnight.

For in vitro release test, three phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with pH values of 5.0, 6.0 and 7.4 were prepared. 0.2g ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were immersed in 50mL PBS at 37°C under orbital shaking with a rotation rate of 80rpm. The DOX-release medium (5mL) was extracted at given time intervals, and was replaced with fresh PBS. The concentration of DOX-release medium was detected by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-4600).

The morphologies and elements of ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM; JSM-6380LV) and energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS), respectively. The mesoporous structure and microstructure of ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL2100) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED). The crystalline phases of ZSM-5 zeolites, CS powders and ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were detected by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD; D/max-III C). The functional groups in the above samples were characterized in the range of wavenumbers 4000–400cm−1 by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR; Nicolet 5DX) using the KBr pellet technique. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks were characterized by an automatic surface area and porosity analyzer (AUTOSORB-1-C, Quantachrome) at 77K. The pore size distribution curves were determined according to the desorption branches by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of hBMSCs and cell culture
ce:section.ce:para: Human bone marrow derived mesenchymal stromal cells (hBMSCs) were harvested from adult bone marrow aspirate, isolated using a Ficoll density gradient and then expanded in culture. The hBMSCs (passage 3–5) were used in this study. The hBMSCs were then expanded in Alpha-MEM which was supplemented with 10% FBS for 5days. Characterization and clinical efficacy of these HBMSCs has been previously reported. The MG63 osteoblastic human osteosarcoma-derived cell line and was cultured with High Glucose DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco), 100U/mL of penicillin, and 100μg/mL streptomycin at 37°C under an atmosphere of 5% (v/v) CO2 in air. The cells were sub-cultured every 2days.

The following anti-human antibodies were used in the experiments: CD29, CD34, CD44, CD90, and HLA-DR (Biolegend), for MSC analysis. Cytometry was performed using a FACS ScanW analyzer (BD Biosciences, San Jose, USA) and the CellquestProW Software (BD Biosciences, San Jose, USA). Positive fluorescence was defined as any event above the background fluorescence, which was defined by a histogram cut-off where 99.5% of the events in isotype antibody labelled cells were considered negative. All results regarding flow cytometry are displayed as % positive cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Osteogenic induction and alizarin red staining
ce:section.ce:para: Cells were seeded onto 48-well plates, grown to ~70% confluence, and incubated in the differentiation medium containing 10−2 M β‑sodium glycerophosphate, 50μg/mL l-ascorbic acid and 10−7 M dexamethasone, and 10% FBS (FBS, Gibco) for 2weeks. Cultures were fixed in 60% isopropanol, and mineralization of extracellular matrix stained with 1% alizarin red.

The effect of DOX on the proliferation of MG63 cells and hBMSCs was evaluated by a Cell Counting Assay Kit-8 (CCK-8). Cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 5.0×103 cells/well and cultured for 24h. Then the culture medium was replaced with 100μL of fresh media containing different concentrations of DOX, ZSM-5 and ZSM-5/CS/DOX, the MG63 cells were further incubated at 37°C for another 24h and hBMSCs for another 5days. The pH of the medium was adjusted with 1M HCl, and the toxicity of ZSM-5 and ZSM-5/CS/DOX (DOX equivalent, 10μg/mL DOX) to MG63 was detected in different pH media. Thereafter, the medium solution was removed, and 10μL of CCK-8 reagent diluted with 90μL of fresh medium was added to each well. The cells were incubated at 37°C for another 2h, and then in order to dissolve formazan crystals, the plate was gently shaken for 2min. Finally, the culture medium was collected in a 96-well plate and measured with enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) at a wavelength of 450nm.

MG63 cells were cultured with DOX, ZSM-5 and ZSM-5/CS/DOX (DOX equivalent, 10μg/mL DOX) on cover glass slides at 37°C in the same manner as above-mentioned. After incubation at 37°C for another 12h and 24h, the plate was washed with PBS for 3 times. The cells were then fixed by 2% paraformaldehyde at 4°C for 25min and washed with PBS for 3 times. Next, 0.2% Triton X-100 was added and kept at 4°C for 2min. Subsequently, cell nuclei were stained with 10μg/mL Hoechst 33258 (Sigma) for 5min. Finally, the stained samples were mounted with 90% glycerinum. Cell nuclei were observed with a Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM).

MG63 were seeded on cover glass slides placed in 6 wells plates (1.0×105 cells/well) and incubated in an incubator for 24h in preparation for confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) analysis. After incubation, the media was exchanged for fresh media containing ZSM-5/CS/DOX (DOX equivalent, 10μg/mL DOX), then incubated for 24h. The culture media were removed, the cells after being rinsed two times with PBS were fixed with formaldehyde, and the cell nuclei were stained with 4′, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, blue). CLSM images of cells were obtained using confocal microscope (TCS SP2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pharmacokinetics and cardiotoxicity study in normal rat
ce:section.ce:para: The in vivo behaviour was studied in normal male Sprague-Dawley rat (average weight 260±20g) by injecting ZSM-5/CS/DOX (DOX equivalent, 5mg/kg DOX) and free DOX (5mg/kg) in the tail vein. All animal surgical procedures were approved by the Animal Research Committee of Shanghai Sixth People's Hospital, Shanghai Jiaotong University School of Medicine. Twenty male SD rats were used for animal experiments. Animals were sacrificed at pre-determined time intervals of 30min and 24h post-injection (p.i.) and the heart were removed, weighed and counted, together with samples of blood, in a gamma-counter. The DOX level in blood was monitored in time by drawing 0.1mL blood from the tail vein of SD rat following i.v. injection of ZSM-5/CS/DOX or free DOX. The blood samples were dissolved in 1% Triton X-100 with brief sonication. DOX was extracted by incubating blood samples in HCl (0.75M, 0.6mL) in isopropanol at −20°C overnight. After centrifugation at 14,800rpm for 30min, the fluorescence of the supernatant was measured using FluoroMax-4 (HORIBA JobinYvon). For biodistribution studies, the rat were sacrificed 24h after injection of ZSM-5/CS/DOX or free DOX (dosage 5mg/kg). The heart (0.1g) was wet-weighed. After strong vortexing and centrifugation, 1ml of the supernatant was extracted and the fluorescence of the supernatant was measured. The blood samples were centrifuged at 4000rpm for 10min to collect serum. Animal serum creatine kinase (CK), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and malondialdehyde (MDA) were determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and porous structure of mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks
ce:section.ce:para: The mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks as drug delivery systems were fabricated according to the following steps: (i) synthesis of ZSM-5 nanodisks by hydrothermal method; (ii) formation of mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks by desilication treatment; and (iii) deposition of CS and DOX on the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks. The SEM and TEM images indicated that the ZSM-5 zeolites presented disk-like shape with thicknesses of 100nm and diameters of 300nm (Fig. 1 ). The mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were converted from the ZSM-5 nanodisks after the desilication treatment, followed by the deposition of CS and DOX. The morphology and particle size of the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks are similar to the pure ZSM-5 zeolites (Figs. 1 and 2a). The EDS pattern in Fig. 2b showed that the chemical elements of the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were composed of Si, Al, O and C. The Si and Al elements were derived from the ZSM-5 zeolites, the O element was derived from the ZSM-5, CS and DOX, and the C element was originated from both the CS and DOX. Notably, lots of light spots within the nanodisks suggested the presence of mesopores, which were created during alkaline desilication process (Fig. 2c). In addition, a CS layer was detected on the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks (Fig. 2d), which not only increase the drug loading efficiency but also made the drug carriers possess the pH-responsive performance for the DOX controlled release. Under the high-resolution TEM image, the crystal lattices with a spacing of 1.1nm were attributed to (200) planes of ZSM-5 zeolites (Fig. 2e). The SAED patter taken from a typical nanodisks indicated the single-crystalline structure with a [010] zone axis (Fig. 2f).

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks was identified as IV Type isotherm with a Type H3 hysteresis loop (Fig. 3a) [55]. The loop did not show any limiting adsorption at high P/Po =0.9–1.0, which might be ascribed to the disordered mesoporous structure (Figs. 2c and 3a). The BJH pore size distribution curve of the ZSM-5 nanodisks indicated that the mesopore size was distributed around 3.75nm (Fig. 3b). Because of the mesoporous and microporous structures, the ZSM-5 nanodisks possessed great surface areas of 465m3/g and pore volumes of 0.40cm3/g. The hierarchically porous structures of the zeolites contributed to enhance their drug delivery property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and porous structure of mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 showed the XRD patterns of the CS powders, ZSM-5 nanodisks and ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks. Only a broad peak was detected in the XRD pattern of the CS because of its semi-crystalline material (Fig. 4a). Notably, the characteristic peaks of ZSM-5 zeolites were observed in both the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks and mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks (Fig. 4b and c), which were ascribed to the type MFI [56]. Because the percentages of the CS and DOX in the mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were lower than the ZSM-5 zeolites, their characteristic peaks could not be detected in Fig. 4c.

Fig. 5 showed the FTIR spectra of the mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks by using the CS powders, mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks as control groups. The broad peak due to the OH or NH groups in the CS powders located at around 3451cm−1 [57,58]. Fig. 5a showed the amide-I vibration (1651cm−1), NH deformation (1602cm−1) and NH wagging in amino groups (895cm−1). The bands at 1323 and 1263cm−1 were attributed to the CN in the secondary amide groups, while those at 1425 and 1386cm−1 were ascribed to the CN in the primary amide groups [57,58]. In addition, the characteristic bands due to the CO stretching vibration and bridge oxygen stretching located at 1092 and 1160cm−1, respectively [57,58]. Fig. 5b and c revealed that the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks and HM-ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks possessed the similar characteristic peaks. The ZSM-5 zeolites were typical siliceous materials, whose characteristic peaks due to the external asymmetric stretch, inter asymmetric stretch, external symmetric stretch and TO bend located at 1228, 1110, 800 and 454cm−1, respectively [57,59]. The band at 550cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric stretching mode of double five rings [56,59]. The characteristic peaks due to CS and DOX were overlapped by those of ZSM-5 zeolites (Fig. 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug delivery performances of mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks
ce:section.ce:para:
For loading the DOX drugs in carriers, the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks were immersed in the CS and DOX mixed solutions followed by adjusting the pH value to 7.4. The mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks exhibited great drug loading efficiency of 97.7%, and the corresponding drug loading amount arrived at 141.7μg/g. The in vitro drug release properties of the mesoporousZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were performed by immersing them in PBS with different pH values at 37°C. The DOX concentrations of the release media were determined by fluorescence spectrophotometer. At the first 72h, the DOX drugs were rapidly released from the drug carriers, and then arrived gradually at drug release equilibrium (Fig. 6a and b). Interestingly, the mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks exhibited pH-responsive performances. In the PBS with pH value of 7.4 that simulated the environment of blood circulation, the mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks exhibited the low DOX cumulative release ratio of only 39.9% within 7days of incubation. In contrast, the cumulative release ratio of DOX from the drug carriers reached 71.0% within 7days when the pH value of PBS was 5.5 that simulated the acidic environment of tumor tissue. In the PBS with pH value of 6.0, the cumulative release ratio of DOX was 58.7%. The above results suggested that the release rates of DOX increased at any time point with the decrease of pH values of drug release media. The drug release kinetics of the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks were demonstrated according to the Korsmeyer-Peppas model [60,61].
(1)
C = a + K ×
t
1 / n
where C was drug cumulative release concentration, t was drug release time, and n, a and K were kinetics constants. In the PBS with pH values of 5.5, 6.5 and 7.4, the corresponding fitted equations for the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks were expressed as:
(2)
C = − 23.90 + 29.05
t
1 / 4
(3)
C = − 22.29 + 25.16
t
1 / 4
(4)
C = − 11.96 + 16.49
t
1 / 4

The release trends of DOX from the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks were fitted well with the drug release kinetics model (Fig. 6c), suggesting that the drug release kinetics was controlled by a diffusion process. When the pH values of PBS were 5.5, 6.5 and 7.4, the K values were 29.05, 25.16 and 16.49h−1/4, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and cytotoxicity assay of hBMSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The hBMSCs was identified by flow cytometry. As consistent with criteria defined by the ISCT, all cells exhibited an immunophenotypic profile typical for hBMSCs. The main hBMSCs surface markers CD24, CD90 and CD44 were high expressed (mean proportion of cells positive for the respective markers ≥97.8%), whereas the expression of HLA-DR and CD34 was virtually absent (mean proportion of positive cells for each of the respective markers positive cells) (Fig. 7a). After 2weeks of osteogenic induction, alizarin red staining showed that hBMSCs formed a large number of calcium nodules (Fig. 7b). Together, these data clearly demonstrated that the isolated cells were indeed hBMSCs.

The hBMSCs were co-cultured with the free DOX, ZSM-5 nanodisks, and ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks for 5days. On days 1 and 5, CCK-8 was used to detect cell viability. There was a significant difference between the free DOX and ZSM-5 groups on the first day, as the cell activity of the hBMSCs in the free DOX group decreased significantly compared to the ZSM-5 group. On day 5, the activities of the hBMSCs co-cultured with the free DOX group were significantly lower than that of the ZSM-5 and ZSM-5/CS/DOX groups (Fig. 7c). The results suggest that the toxicity of the ZSM-5 and ZSM-5/CS/DOX groups to the hBMSCs was less than that of the DOX group and ZSM-5 on the proliferation of the hBMSCs.

DOX exerts a broad spectrum of antitumor activity, through the cell membrane, into the nucleus, interfering with tumor cells DNA, to inhibit the proliferation of tumor cells [62,63,64]. Therefore, DOX entering cells is a prerequisite for antitumor action. After 24h of co-culture with ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks (DOX equivalent, 10μg/mL), the blue nuclei of MG63 are surrounded by a large number of red fluorophores. This suggested that ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were well absorbed by cells and aggregates in MG63 cells, released DOX to performed anti-tumor effects (Fig. 8 ).

DOX causes a broad spectrum of antitumor activity, through the cell membrane, into the nucleus, interfering with the tumor cells' DNA, to inhibit the proliferation of tumor cells [62,63,64]. Therefore, DOX entering the cells is a prerequisite for antitumor action. After 24h of co-culture with the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks (DOX equivalent, 10μg/mL), the blue nuclei of theMG63 cells were surrounded by a large number of red fluorophores. This suggests that the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were well absorbed by the cells and aggregated in the MG63 cells, releasing DOX to carry out its anti-tumor effects (Fig. 8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity studies of MG63
ce:section.ce:para: The study on the cytotoxicity of drug delivery vehicles is very important to drug delivery systems. The in vitro cytotoxicity of the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were estimated using a CCK-8 assay, and compared with the ZSM-5 and free DOX groups. As shown in Fig. 9a, MG63 cells were incubated in various DOX concentrations (ranging from 0.5 to 10μg/mL) in the 96-well plates for 1day. According to the graph data, the ZSM-5 without DOX group did not cause drastic cytotoxicity in the MG63 cells within the range of 0.5–10μg/mL. With increased concentrations, the cytotoxicity of the ZSM-5/CS/DOX increased and was always performed better than the same concentration of DOX.

The pH-responsive ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks had different drug release rates in different pH environments. Therefore, we also explored the toxicity of ZSM-5 and ZSM-5/CS/DOX (DOX equiv.10μg/mL) to tumor cells in different pH environments. The MG63 cells were cultured in pH=7.4, 6.0, and 5.5 medias for 1day with ZSM-5 nanodisks and ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks, while CCK-8 was used to detect cell viability. As shown in Fig. 3b, the ZSM-5 group had no effect on the MG63 cells activity in different pH environments. The ZSM-5nanodisks had good cell compatibility in different environments. However, within a pH=5.5 environment, the toxicity of the ZSM-5/CS/DOX to the MG63 cells was higher than that in a pH=7.4 scenario (p <0.05). This shows that ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks have good pH response, is able to release more DOX in an acidic environment, and inhibits the MG63 cells. (Fig. 9b).

CLSM was employed to observe the morphology of the cell nuclei in this study to detect cell apoptosis. Cells treated with the ZSM-5nanodisks showed similar nuclei features to those cultured in the control group and the number of cells increased steadily along with the increase in culture time. When the MG63 cells were directly exposed to ZSM-5/CS/DOX, the cell nuclei appeared to deform and be crushed after incubation for 12h and 24h because of its severe toxicity, exhibiting serious cell apoptosis and death [65]. A similar phenomenon was also observed in the MG63 cells treated with the free DOX. These results again confirmed that DOX can be efficiently released from ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks in cells' endosomes/lysosomes to result in cell apoptosis (Fig. 10 ).

The plasma levels of DOX were determined to be similar to a previous report through fluorescence spectroscopy at different time intervals following a single intravenous injection of ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks (5mg/kg DOX equiv.) in the SD rats [66,67]. Notably, the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks revealed a significantly longer circulation time than that of the free DOX (p <0.05). The concentration of DOX in plasma decreased to undetectable levels 0.5h after the injection of the free DOX, whereas considerable amounts of DOX was observed even 24h after the administration of ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks (Fig. 11a).

After the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks and free DOX were injected in the SD rats, respectively, the biodistribution of the DOX was determined at 24h. Fig. 11b shows that less DOX was accumulated in the heart for the ZSM-5/CS/DOX group than that for the free DOX group (p <0.05), suggesting that the controlled DOX release from the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks could effectively reduce the toxicity conveyed to the heart. ZSM-5/CS/DOX can prevent the DOX concentration in blood from rapidly decreasing, and continue to produce toxicity to the tumor cells. In the HE stained sections of the heart, we also found that, compared to DOX, ZSM-5/CS/DOX caused fewer incidences of myocardial degeneration, and that the ZSM-5 group had no effect on the cardiomyocytes.

To further detect the severity of the cardiotoxicity in the free DOX and ZSM-5/CS/DOX groups, we selectively used characteristic serological indicators to detect for heart damage. The ZSM-5/CS/DOX group caused less CK and MDA rise compared to the free DOX group (Fig. 12a and b, p <0.05), but there was no significant difference in LDH between the two groups. (Fig. 12c).

Osteosarcoma is the most frequent bone tumor observed clinically. The standard treatment for conventional osteosarcoma is based on pre- and postoperative chemotherapy, including DOX, cisplatin and methotrexate. This treatment is successful in about 55%–60% of patients. Although many researchers have sought to find new treatments for osteosarcoma, the prognosis of osteosarcoma patients has not improved over the past few decades [68,69]. The major drawback of DOX is the occurrence of resistance, which gradually leads to chemotherapy ineffective [69] and the onset of cardiotoxicity [70]. Herein, mesoporous ZSM-5/CS nanodisks were fabricated for drug delivery systems, and the controlled release of DOX improves the therapeutic efficacy at tumor-targeted site without systemic toxicity (Fig. 13 ).

The fabrication steps of the mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks included: (i) synthesis of ZSM-5 nanodisks by hydrothermal method; (ii) formation of mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks by desilication treatment; and (iii) deposition of CS and DOX on the mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks. The as-prepared ZSM-5 zeolites by hydrothermal method presented the disk-like shape with thicknesses of 100nm and diameters of 300nm (Fig. 1). After the desilication treatment, the mesopores with pore size of 3.75nm was produced within the nanodisks (Fig. 3). The DOX drugs were loaded in the carriers by the immersion of mesoporous ZSM-5 nanodisks in the CS and DOX mixed solutions, followed by adjusting the pH value to 7.4 (Fig. 2). Interestingly, the drug efficiency and drug loading amount of the mesoporous ZSM-5/CS nanodisks arrived at 97.7% and 141.7μg/g, respectively. The excellent drug loaded property of the above drug carriers were mainly attributed to the following reasons. On one hand, the mesoporous structures in the ZSM-5 zeolites provided drug delivery channels and great specific surface areas for loading drug molecules [71]; on the other hand, the functional groups in CS improved the drug loading property of DOX via hydrogen bonding interaction. Fig. 6 indicated that the mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks presented the controlled release performance in the drug release media with pH values of 5.5, 6.0 and 7.4. The drug release trends of the DOX from the drug carriers were fitted well with the Korsmeyer-Peppas model (Fig. 6c), suggesting that the DOX release kinetics was determined by a diffusion process. The PBS with pH values of 7.4 and 5.5 simulated the environment of blood circulation and tumor tissue, respectively. The mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks exhibited pH-responsive performances. At 7days of incubation, the DOX cumulative release ratio form the carriers was only 39.9% at pH=7.4, while that reached 71.0% at pH=5.5 (Fig. 6).

The value of the extracellular pH in malignant tumors ranged from 5.8 to 7.6, compared with 7.4 in the blood [72]. So, the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks also released DOX into the extracellular space before internalization. Interestingly, the use of DOX to decrease tumor cell density and increase interstitial space could promote nanoparticle infiltration in the three-dimensional environment of the tumors [73,74]. Meanwhile, the more the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were aggregated, the better antitumor effects they had after being phagocytosised by the tumor cells.

Cytotoxicity is a direct index of anti-tumor activity evaluation. Considering DOX's action mechanism, a high accumulation of DOX in tumor cells might be related to a high toxicity. DOX is an intracellular chemotherapy agent, so the intracellular accumulation of DOX in tumor cells can be seen as an effective dose of cytotoxicity [75]. In our current study, the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks had a good ability of intracellular aggregation through fluorescent localization. The ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks delivered drugs into the cytoplasm through endocytosis and endosomlysis, overcoming the MDR. The high intracellular concentration of DOX might be a premise for good toxicity against tumor cells and enhancing anti-tumor activity in vivo, which indicates that our designed conjugate will be more toxic than the free DOX.

In the in vivo experiments, we found that, compared with the free DOX, the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks remained longer in the serum. This proves that ZSM-5/CS/DOX has stronger antitumor ability. Meanwhile, the most severe side effect of DOX, cardiotoxicity, was also examined. In the ZSM-5/CS/DOX group, the DOX levels in the heart were lower and the heart slices showed less cardiotoxicity compared to the free DOX treatment group. DOX's cardiotoxicity limits its clinical use, and many research studies on the cardiotoxicity of DOX have been conducted in recent years. More attention has been focused on nano-drug delivery systems to maintain effective concentrations of DOX while avoiding severe cardiotoxicity. In this study, the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks guaranteed effective blood concentration, while the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks simultaneously had a unique pH response that released less DOX to the heart and reduced the side effects of DOX. CK is an important kinase in the cytoplasm and mitochondria of the heart, muscle and brain tissues of animals, which is directly related to cellular energy processing, muscle contractions, and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) regeneration, as it reversibly catalyzes the phosphoryl shift between creatine and ATP. The levels of CK increase rapidly when myocardial infarction occur [76,77]. MDA is the main damage that results in membrane lipid peroxidation damage to the bio membrane structure, and changes to membrane permeability, which affects a series of physiological and biochemical reactions [78,79]. Compared with the free DOX, the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks caused less CK and MDA elevation, better protecting the heart.

More research should be done on the in vivo behaviors of ZSM-5/CS/DOX, including clearance, long-term toxicology, and mechanism of endocytosis. Moreover, it would be worthwhile to investigate optimization of the drug delivery to make it more suitable for in vivo cancer treatment.

In summary, we first reported the synthesis of the mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks as drug delivery systems against osteosarcoma. The mesopores within theZSM-5 nanodisks provided the space for loading the DOX drugs, while the CS layer on the nanodisks could regulate the DOX release. The release of DOX from the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks was heavily dependent on the pH values. It was discovered that DOX is released rapidly in acidic pH environments, such as those encountered in tumor tissue. The therapeutic effects of the ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks were investigated with both in vivo and in vitro tests. Compared to the free DOX group, the mesoporousZSM-5/CS/DOX group possessed higher abilities to suppress MG63 cells without resulting in systemic toxicity. Therefore, the mesoporous ZSM-5/CS/DOX nanodisks are promising drug delivery systems for use in cancer therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 51372152), Innovation Foundation of Shanghai Education Committee (no. 14ZZ124).
